read-history-with-log-and-show.label: Read History with Log and Show
read-history-with-log-and-show.summary: Read the history overview, open one checkpoint, and select the current or previous snapshot with HEAD notation.

sketch0.title: Use the Log as a Table of Contents

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Scan the One-Line History
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Read the timeline from newest checkpoint to oldest.

help.concept: git log --oneline compresses each snapshot to an identifier and subject so the project story is easy to scan.
help.hint_1: Focus on commit order and subject lines rather than machine-specific hashes.
help.hint_2: The newest entry should describe the result from this milestone.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.starterCode: Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Latest Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Use `git show --stat HEAD` to see the latest commit message and the files it changed.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Open the checkpoint named in the task and inspect its evidence.

help.concept: git show connects a commit’s message to the exact snapshot and patch it introduced.
help.hint_1: Choose a summary view for file shape or a full patch for line-level detail.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the snapshot contents support its message.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Previous Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The newest commit changed the README. Inspect `HEAD~1` to review the snapshot immediately before it.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Open the checkpoint named in the task and inspect its evidence.

help.concept: HEAD~1 names the first parent of the current commit, which is the snapshot immediately before HEAD in this linear history.
help.hint_1: Choose a summary view for file shape or a full patch for line-level detail.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the snapshot contents support its message.

single-choice-1.title: HEAD in Git
single-choice-1.prompt: What does `HEAD` refer to in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The first commit in the repository
options.c: The latest commit in the current branch

multi-choice-1.title: Understanding git log --oneline
multi-choice-1.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

options.a: A detailed diff of each commit
options.b: A single line summary of each commit
options.c: The full commit message and author details

multi-choice-2.title: Purpose of git show
multi-choice-2.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git show` command?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

options.a: To list all commits in a branch
options.b: To show detailed information about a specific commit
options.c: To display the current branch name

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Commands
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the following Git commands in the order you would use them to inspect a commit's history and details.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

sketch0.title: Read the Timeline
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The one-line log is the table of contents for repository history:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

Each row pairs a shortened identifier with a subject, newest first. This is the one introduction to `git log`; later projects will use it as an audit step.

sketch1.title: Open One Checkpoint
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
`git show` opens one recorded snapshot. A statistics view gives a compact shape—subject, files, and line counts:

```bash
git show --stat HEAD
```

Use it when the timeline points to a commit that deserves closer inspection.

sketch2.title: Select the Current or Previous Snapshot
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
`HEAD` names the commit currently checked out. `HEAD~1` names its first parent—the snapshot immediately before it.

These names let you move from the timeline to a specific checkpoint without copying a generated identifier. They select history; they do not change it.
